metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: Explorations in Social Studies 11 is a flexible course that allows students to explore a range of social studies topics aligned with provincial learning standards. Students choose from at least three content areas covering topics such as Asia's economic rise, BC First Peoples cultures, urbanization, law, philosophy, economic theory, genocide studies, religious traditions, social justice, and political systems. The course builds critical inquiry skills applicable across all social studies disciplines.
metadata.general_description: A flexible Grade 11 course allowing students to explore diverse social studies topics including Indigenous cultures, political systems, economics, philosophy, law, urban studies, genocide studies, and world religions.
metadata.course_structure_note: Students must demonstrate learning standards in at least THREE of the listed content areas. Schools and teachers may choose which areas to offer.

big_ideas.statement: Rapid industrialization, urbanization, and economic growth in Asia in the late 20th century have created complex social, political, and environmental challenges.
big_ideas.elaboration: Asian economies have become global powerhouses, but growth has come with inequality, environmental strain, and political tension.

big_ideas.statement: Cultural expressions convey the richness, diversity, and resiliency of B.C. First Peoples.
big_ideas.elaboration: Language, art, ceremony, and oral tradition are living expressions of First Peoples identity, resilience, and connection to land.

big_ideas.statement: Understanding the diversity and complexity of cultural expressions in one culture enhances our understanding of other cultures.
big_ideas.elaboration: Deep engagement with one cultural tradition builds tools for cross-cultural empathy and comparative understanding.

big_ideas.statement: Physical features and natural resources influence demographic patterns and population distribution.
big_ideas.elaboration: Where people live, how they move, and how they build economies is profoundly shaped by the geography of their environment.

big_ideas.statement: Understanding how political decisions are made is critical to being an informed and engaged citizen.
big_ideas.elaboration: Democracy requires citizens who understand systems of power, electoral processes, lobbying, and policy making.

big_ideas.statement: The rapid development and proliferation of communication and transportation technologies in the 20th century led to profound changes in personal and national identities.
big_ideas.elaboration: Radio, television, the internet, and global travel compressed the world and reshaped what it means to belong to a community or nation.

big_ideas.statement: Indigenous peoples are reclaiming mental, emotional, physical, and spiritual well-being despite the continuing effects of colonialism.
big_ideas.elaboration: Through cultural revitalization, land rights advocacy, and community development, Indigenous peoples are healing from historical trauma and asserting self-determination.

big_ideas.statement: Religion can powerfully shape social, political, legal, and environmental values.
big_ideas.elaboration: Religious belief systems influence governance, law, environmental stewardship, gender roles, and social norms across cultures and centuries.

big_ideas.statement: The implementation of economic theories has profound effects on social and political decision making and movements.
big_ideas.elaboration: Whether a society adopts capitalism, Keynesianism, Marxism, or participatory economics shapes everything from labour rights to welfare policy to international trade.

big_ideas.statement: The intentional destruction of peoples and their cultures is not inevitable, and such attempts can be disrupted and resisted.
big_ideas.elaboration: Genocide is not a natural disaster — it is a political process that can be predicted, intervened in, and prevented.

big_ideas.statement: A society's laws and legal framework affect many aspects of people's daily lives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Laws shape everything from family structure to economic opportunity, and legal systems reflect the values and power structures of their societies.

big_ideas.statement: Examining questions in philosophy allows people to question their assumptions and better understand their own beliefs.
big_ideas.elaboration: Philosophical inquiry develops intellectual humility, critical reasoning, and the capacity to engage with radically different worldviews.

big_ideas.statement: Social justice initiatives can transform individuals and systems.
big_ideas.elaboration: Activism, advocacy, and community organizing have driven real changes in law, culture, and institutions — and continue to do so.

big_ideas.statement: Decision making in urban and regional planning requires balancing political, economic, social, and environmental factors.
big_ideas.elaboration: Cities are shaped by planning decisions that affect housing affordability, transportation, sustainability, and quality of life.

categories.name: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills
categories.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills to ask questions; gather, interpret, and analyze ideas; and communicate findings and decisions.

competencies.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes to ask questions, gather and analyze ideas, and communicate findings.
i_can_statements: I can develop my own inquiry questions about a social studies topic.
I can gather information from multiple types of sources and assess their credibility.
I can analyze complex data and communicate my conclusions in an appropriate format.
I can lead or participate constructively in a group investigation.

categories.name: Significance
categories.raw: Assess the significance of people, places, events, phenomena, ideas, or developments.

competencies.raw: Assess the significance of people, places, events, phenomena, ideas, or developments.
i_can_statements: I can apply criteria to evaluate why something matters in a given context.
I can compare how different groups assign different levels of significance to the same thing.
I can defend a position on why something is or isn't historically or socially significant.

categories.name: Evidence
categories.raw: Assess the credibility and justifiability of evidence, data, and interpretations.

competencies.raw: Assess the credibility and justifiability of evidence, data, and interpretations.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate whether a piece of evidence is credible based on its source, context, and purpose.
I can compare competing interpretations of data and explain which is better justified.
I can identify logical fallacies and unsupported claims in arguments.

categories.name: Continuity and Change
categories.raw: Compare and contrast continuities and changes, trends and patterns, or similarities and differences for different people, places, events, phenomena, ideas, or developments.

competencies.raw: Compare and contrast continuities and changes, trends and patterns for different people or developments.
i_can_statements: I can trace how a social issue, institution, or idea has changed over time.
I can identify patterns across different cultures or time periods.
I can explain why some things change quickly while others are slow to shift.

categories.name: Cause and Consequence
categories.raw: Assess the short- and long-term causes and expected and unexpected consequences of people's actions, events, phenomena, ideas, or developments.

competencies.raw: Assess the short- and long-term causes and expected and unexpected consequences of actions and developments.
i_can_statements: I can identify the most important causes of a social phenomenon.
I can distinguish between consequences that were intended and those that were not.
I can trace how a single decision or event set off a chain of consequences.

categories.name: Perspective
categories.raw: Infer and explain different perspectives on people, places, events, phenomena, ideas, or developments.

competencies.raw: Infer and explain different perspectives on people, places, events, or developments.
i_can_statements: I can infer how someone from a different culture or background would view an issue.
I can explain how worldview, values, and lived experience shape a person's perspective.
I can present multiple perspectives on a controversial topic fairly and accurately.

categories.name: Ethical Judgment
categories.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about people, places, events, phenomena, ideas, or developments and determine appropriate ways to respond.

competencies.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments and determine appropriate ways to respond.
i_can_statements: I can make a reasoned ethical argument about a contemporary or historical issue.
I can consider context, competing values, and real-world consequences in my ethical reasoning.
I can propose a thoughtful response to an injustice — individual, community, or systemic.

content.structure_note: Students must engage with at least THREE of the following content areas.

areas.name: Social, Cultural, or Technological Innovations in the 20th Century
areas.raw: Social, cultural, or technological innovations and developments in the 20th century.
topics: Communication technologies: radio, television, the internet
Transportation revolutions: automobiles, commercial aviation
Shifts in gender roles and family structures
The civil rights movement and social justice movements
Globalization and cultural exchange

areas.name: Colonialism and Contemporary Issues for Indigenous People
areas.raw: Colonialism and contemporary issues for indigenous people in Canada and around the world.
topics: The legacy of residential schools
Land rights, treaty rights, and self-determination
Missing and murdered Indigenous women and girls (MMIWG)
Indigenous cultural revitalization movements
Global Indigenous rights: the UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples

areas.name: Current Issues in Local, Regional, National, or Global Politics
areas.raw: Current issues in local, regional, national, or global politics as represented in mass media.
topics: Media literacy and political reporting
Canadian federal and provincial political issues
Global political conflicts and diplomacy
The role of social media in politics
Voter engagement and democratic participation

areas.name: Natural Resource Use and Development
areas.raw: Natural resource use and local, regional, national, or global development.
topics: BC resource industries: forestry, mining, fisheries, LNG
Environmental impacts of resource extraction
Indigenous perspectives on resource development
Sustainable development and green economy
Global resource competition and geopolitics

areas.name: Economic Development in Asia
topics: The rise of China as a global economic power
The 'Asian Tigers': South Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, Hong Kong
India's economic development and challenges
Manufacturing, globalization, and labour conditions in Asia
Environmental consequences of rapid industrialization

areas.name: Systems of Power and Governance in Global Cultures
areas.raw: Systems of power and governance in global cultures.
topics: Types of government: democracy, theocracy, authoritarianism
Political structures in the developing world
International governance: UN, NATO, WTO
Power and resistance: social movements and activism
Gender and power in governance systems

areas.name: Diversity of BC First Peoples Territories and Communities
areas.raw: Diversity of B.C. First Peoples territories and communities.
topics: Traditional territories of BC First Nations
Linguistic and cultural diversity among BC Indigenous peoples
Contemporary First Peoples communities: urban and rural
Cultural expressions: art, ceremony, language, storytelling
Land and identity: the relationship between place and culture

areas.name: Rights of Individuals in Canada
topics: The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms
Human rights law in Canada
The right to protest and civil disobedience
Rights of marginalized groups: Indigenous, LGBTQ+, racialized communities
Balancing individual rights with collective rights

areas.name: Representations of Natural and Human-Made Phenomena
areas.raw: Representations of natural and human-made phenomena.
topics: Geographic and scientific literacy: maps, data, and models
How media represents disasters and crises
Data visualization in the social sciences
Spatial thinking and geographic information systems (GIS)
The built environment and human geography

areas.name: Methods Used to Promote Social Justice
areas.raw: Methods used by individuals, groups, and organizations to promote social justice.
topics: Activism and protest movements
Non-governmental organizations (NGOs) and advocacy
Art and culture as tools for social change
Legal strategies: litigation, charters, and human rights tribunals
Community organizing and grassroots movements

areas.name: Fundamental Nature of Knowledge, Existence, and Reality
areas.raw: Fundamental nature of knowledge, existence, and reality.
topics: Introduction to epistemology: what can we know?
Metaphysics: the nature of reality and existence
Logic and argumentation
Philosophy of mind: consciousness and identity
Ethics and moral philosophy

areas.name: Sacred Texts, Traditions, and Narratives of Cultures
areas.raw: Sacred texts, traditions, and narratives of cultures.
topics: World religious texts: the Bible, Quran, Torah, Bhagavad Gita, etc.
Oral traditions as sacred knowledge
Mythology and cultural narrative
Ritual, ceremony, and religious practice
Religion and cultural identity

areas.name: Contemporary Theories of Money and Investment
areas.raw: Contemporary theories of money and investment.
topics: Basics of macroeconomics: supply, demand, inflation, interest rates
Investment strategies: stocks, bonds, real estate
Central banking and monetary policy
Cryptocurrency and alternative financial systems
Economic inequality and wealth distribution

areas.name: Recognition of and Responses to Genocide
areas.raw: Recognition of and responses to genocide.
topics: The definition of genocide (UN Convention, 1948)
Historical genocides: the Holocaust, Rwanda, Cambodia, Armenia
Warning signs and stages of genocide
International legal responses: the ICC and tribunals
Memory, memorialization, and prevention

areas.name: Global Issues in Urbanization
topics: Global urbanization trends
Megacities and their challenges
Housing affordability and homelessness
Sustainable urban design and planning
Urbanization in the developing world
